title: Generation of Eddy Currents by an AC Field
narrationScript: Eddy current testing begins with a coil and an alternating current. Drive the coil with AC and its magnetic field expands, collapses, and reverses every cycle — a field that never holds still. Faraday's law says a changing magnetic flux induces a voltage in any conductor it reaches, and the faster the change, the larger the voltage. Bring a conductive part into that field and the induced voltage drives electrons around closed circular loops. Those loops are eddy currents. Lenz's law sets their direction — at every instant they flow opposite to the coil current, and their own field opposes the field that created them. Think of it as a transformer. The coil is the primary winding, and the part acts as a single short-circuited secondary turn, coupled by mutual induction with no contact and no couplant. Three conditions, no exceptions: a changing field, a conductive material, and coupling between coil and part. Remove any one, and the currents — and your signal — vanish.

sections.heading: An Alternating Current Makes a Field That Never Holds Still
sections.body:
Module 1 left you with the two facts this entire method stands on: a current through a coil produces a magnetic field, and a *changing* magnetic field induces a voltage in any conductor it reaches — Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction. Eddy current testing is simply those two facts put to work on purpose.

Drive a test coil with **alternating current** and its magnetic field — the **primary field** — expands, collapses, and reverses polarity every half-cycle. Nothing about it ever holds still. At a test frequency of 100 kHz the flux runs through 100,000 complete cycles every second, and Faraday's law pays for exactly that: the induced voltage is proportional to the **rate of change of flux**, not its strength at any frozen instant.

Now contrast direct current. Switch DC into the same coil and the flux changes only during the brief switch-on transient — one momentary pulse of induction, then a strong but perfectly **static** field that induces nothing at all. That is why every eddy current instrument is built around an **AC oscillator**: alternating current keeps the flux changing continuously, at a frequency we choose. Industrial test frequencies run from below 1 kHz for thick sections and ferromagnetic tubing up to several megahertz for surface work on aerospace structure.

sections.heading: Bring a Conductor Into That Field
sections.body:
Place an electrically conductive part within reach of the primary field and induction takes over. The changing flux induces an **electromotive force (EMF)** — an electrical pressure — throughout the volume of material the field penetrates. Metal is full of free electrons and offers closed conducting paths in every direction, so that EMF immediately drives current around **closed circular loops** inside the part. These are **eddy currents**, named for the swirling eddies behind a boulder in a stream — and they are not a figure of speech. Real charge circulates through real metal, reversing direction in step with the coil current for as long as the AC flows.

Two laws govern them completely:

- **Faraday's law sets the magnitude.** More flux change per second — higher frequency, stronger coil current, tighter coupling — means more induced EMF, and a more conductive part lets that EMF drive more current.
- **Lenz's law sets the direction.** Induced current always flows so that its own magnetic field **opposes the change that created it**. At every instant, the eddy currents circulating beneath the coil flow **opposite** to the coil current.

That opposition is not a nuisance — it is the entire basis of the measurement, as the next two lessons will show.

sections.caption: From AC to eddy currents: the coil's alternating flux crosses the air gap into the part (side view), inducing circular loops that mirror the coil winding in the opposite direction (top view). The strip below traces the full energy-transfer chain.

sections.heading: The Part Becomes the Secondary of a Transformer
sections.body:
The cleanest model for what is happening is a **transformer**. In a transformer, alternating current in a **primary winding** creates a changing flux that induces current in a **secondary winding** — energy crosses between two circuits that never touch, by **mutual induction**.

An eddy current probe over a conductive part is exactly that machine, minus the iron core:

- The **test coil is the primary** — many turns, driven by the instrument's oscillator.
- The **part is the secondary** — and because the induced loops close on themselves inside solid metal, it behaves as a **single turn that is short-circuited**. A dead short through low-resistance metal carries a healthy current from even a small induced EMF.

Energy transfers across the air gap magnetically — **no electrical contact, no couplant**, no surface preparation beyond reasonable cleanliness. That non-contact coupling is one of the method's defining advantages.

If you doubt the currents are real, look at the same physics with the power turned up: an **induction cooktop** boils water with eddy currents induced in the pan, induction furnaces melt steel with them, and **eddy-current brakes** slow trains and roller coasters with the Lenz's-law opposition you just learned. ET uses the identical mechanism at tiny power levels — we induce currents to *sense* the part, never to change it.

sections.caption: The coil-and-part system is an air-core transformer: a multi-turn primary (the coil) magnetically coupled to a single short-circuited secondary turn (the part). The same physics, with the power turned up, runs cooktops, furnaces, and brakes.

sections.title: Three conditions — no exceptions
sections.body: Eddy currents appear when, and only when, all three are present: **(1) a time-varying magnetic field**, supplied by alternating current in the test coil; **(2) an electrically conductive material** — any metal, plus conductors such as graphite and carbon fiber, but never glass, acrylic, or dry fiberglass; **(3) coupling** — the part must sit close enough that the coil's field actually reaches it, because field strength collapses rapidly with distance. Remove any one and the currents vanish — and with them, every signal this method can produce.

sections.heading: The Shape of the Flow
sections.body:
Eddy currents are not a vague cloud of charge — they have a definite geometry you can predict.

- **They mirror the coil.** The induced loops are circular and concentric, echoing the winding that created them. They flow in planes **parallel to the coil turns** — for a surface probe, parallel to the part surface — and **perpendicular to the primary flux**.
- **They are strongest at the surface, directly beneath the windings.** Current density falls off rapidly with depth — the *skin effect*, which Module 3 puts numbers on — and with radial distance from the coil.
- **Density passes through zero on the coil's axis.** Just as a circular winding has no conductor at its own center, the induced pattern has no current at the center point beneath it. A small flaw sitting exactly on the centerline of a circular probe sits in the one spot the currents ignore — one reason scan patterns overlap.
- **Loops must close inside the conductor.** Current cannot leave the part, cross an insulating boundary, or jump a gap.

Hold that last point. When a surface crack interrupts a loop's path, the current is forced to detour around or beneath it — and that disturbance, as you will see shortly, is precisely what an eddy current instrument detects.

sections.caption: Six bench cases: apply the three conditions and predict whether eddy currents flow.
rows: AC-driven coil resting on an aluminum plate
Changing field, conductive part, tight coupling: all three conditions met
Same coil and plate, steady DC drive
The field is strong but static — constant flux induces nothing
DC-driven coil swept rapidly across the plate
Motion changes the flux through the part — induction needs change, not AC specifically
AC-driven coil on glass, acrylic, or dry fiberglass
No free charge carriers — an insulator gives the induced EMF nothing to drive
AC-driven coil on graphite or carbon-fiber laminate
Carbon conducts — non-metal does not mean non-conductive; CFRP is tested at high frequency
AC-driven coil held 25 mm above the aluminum plate
The primary field collapses rapidly with distance — coupling is lost

sections.heading: From Generation to Signal
sections.body:
Generation is only the first half of a two-way conversation. The eddy currents circulating in the part generate a magnetic field of their own — the **secondary field** — and by Lenz's law it **opposes the primary field** that created it. That opposition reaches back across the air gap and loads the coil, changing the coil's **impedance**: the quantity every eddy current instrument actually measures. How that happens is the business of the next lesson.

What you must own from this lesson is the chain, in order and from memory:

**oscillator → alternating coil current → primary field → changing flux in the part → induced EMF → closed-loop eddy currents**

Every measurement this method makes — conductivity sorting, coating thickness by lift-off, crack detection at a fastener hole, wall thinning in heat-exchanger tubing — reaches the display through that chain. If any link weakens, the signal weakens with it: drop the frequency and the induced EMF falls; pick a poor conductor and the current falls; lift the probe and the coupling falls. You now hold the front half of the method. The back half — how the part talks back to the coil — comes next.

figures.title: Generation of eddy currents by an AC coil field — side view, top view, and energy chain
The transformer analogy: coil as primary, part as shorted secondary
Four bench setups — where are eddy currents flowing?

interactive.title: Where Are Eddy Currents Flowing?
interactive.intro: Four bench setups, four test parts. Apply the three conditions — changing field, conductive material, coupling — and click the ONE part that has eddy currents circulating in it right now.

regions.label: Station 1 plate — AC drive, probe on aluminum
regions.feedback: Correct. All three conditions are met: the AC-driven coil supplies a continuously changing field, aluminum conducts, and the probe rests on the part so coupling is tight. Eddy currents are circulating beneath that coil right now.

regions.label: Station 2 plate — steady DC drive
regions.feedback: No currents here. The DC-fed coil makes a strong field, but strong is not enough — induction requires CHANGING flux. After the brief switch-on transient, a steady field induces nothing.

regions.label: Station 3 plate — AC drive on acrylic
regions.feedback: The field here is changing beautifully, but acrylic is an insulator: no free charge carriers, so the induced EMF has nothing to drive. No conductor, no eddy currents — which is also why ET cannot inspect plastics or glass.

regions.label: Station 4 plate — coil held 25 mm above
regions.feedback: Conductive part, changing field — but the coil's field collapses rapidly with distance, and at 25 mm essentially none of it reaches the aluminum. Coupling is the third condition, and it fails here. That distance sensitivity is the lift-off effect you will later use to measure coating thickness.

checkQuestions.question: Which set of conditions must ALL be present for eddy currents to be generated in a test part?
options: A ferromagnetic part, direct current in the coil, and direct surface contact
A conductive part, a couplant layer, and a steady magnetic field
A time-varying magnetic field, an electrically conductive part, and the part within the coil's field
A time-varying magnetic field, a ferromagnetic part, and relative motion between coil and part
checkQuestions.explanation: Generation requires changing flux (the AC-driven coil), a conductive material for the induced EMF to drive current through, and coupling — the part must actually intercept the field. Ferromagnetism is not required (aluminum tests beautifully), and neither contact nor couplant is ever needed.

checkQuestions.question: Why is the test coil driven with alternating current rather than steady direct current?
options: Only a changing magnetic flux induces a voltage — a steady DC field induces nothing after the switch-on transient
Direct current would overheat the coil windings
Static magnetic fields cannot penetrate metal surfaces
Alternating current is needed to magnetically saturate the part
checkQuestions.explanation: Faraday's law ties the induced EMF to the rate of change of flux. DC builds a strong but constant field, and constant flux induces zero current. (Where magnetic saturation is used on ferromagnetic parts, it is applied with DC or permanent magnets — the opposite of this distractor.)

checkQuestions.question: In the transformer analogy for eddy current testing, the conductive test part plays the role of:
options: the laminated iron core
the primary winding
an open-circuited secondary with many turns
a single-turn secondary winding that is short-circuited
checkQuestions.explanation: The coil is the multi-turn primary; the part behaves as a one-turn secondary shorted on itself, because the induced loops close inside solid metal. Mutual induction couples the two with no electrical contact.

checkQuestions.question: At any instant, the eddy currents induced directly beneath a surface probe coil flow:
options: in the same direction as the coil current, reinforcing the primary field
opposite to the coil current, in closed loops that mirror the winding
radially outward from the coil's center toward the edges of the part
parallel to the primary flux lines, straight down into the part
checkQuestions.explanation: Lenz's law: induced current opposes the change that created it, so the loops mirror the coil winding in the opposite direction and generate a secondary field opposing the primary. The flow is perpendicular to the flux and parallel to the part surface — never radial or straight down.

checkQuestions.question: An AC-driven probe is placed, in turn, on four flat samples. Which one will develop NO eddy currents?
options: A titanium alloy plate
A carbon-fiber (graphite-epoxy) laminate
A dry glass-fiber (fiberglass) panel
An austenitic stainless steel plate
checkQuestions.explanation: Glass fibers and epoxy are insulators — no free charge carriers, so the induced EMF can drive no current. Titanium and austenitic stainless are poor conductors compared with aluminum, but they conduct and are tested routinely; carbon fibers conduct weakly, which is why CFRP can be examined at high frequencies.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105 (2024), ET Level I Topical Outline §2.0–2.1.1 (electromagnetic theory; generation of eddy currents by means of an AC field)
ASNT SNT-TC-1A (2024), Recommended Practice — eddy current method training and qualification framework
NAS 410, NAS Certification and Qualification of Nondestructive Test Personnel (aerospace ET personnel requirements)
ASTM E1316, Standard Terminology for Nondestructive Examinations — Section C, Electromagnetic Testing definitions
